channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - www.gamedeveloper.com
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for www.gamedeveloper.com

item.title: Opinion: Lars Wingefors is out of his mind
item.content:encoded:
Chris Kerr,

Senior Editor, News,GameDeveloper.com

May 21, 2026

6 Min Read

Image via I Think You Should Leave / Netflix

Here's a story I suspect you'll find interesting.

In 2022 I pitched an interview with Embracer co-founder and then-CEO Lars Wingefors. The Swedish conglomerate was in midst of a whopping M&A spree and I thought it would be prudent to chat with the brains behind the operation. When a company is hurling cash at the wall, it's always good to know whether anybody has considered the possibility that some might stick.

I reached out to Wingefors directly, but was swiftly intercepted by an Embracer communications representative. He was a very busy man after all. They told me they would be "happy to plan" for an interview with Lars and suggested we time our chat around the publication of an upcoming interim report. Makes sense. Yet, as always seems to be the case with Embracer, there was a catch.

In that same email, I was informed Embracer would need to be given the option of proofreading the text, making edits, and approving the final draft ahead of publication. No, I am not mincing my words (I have the email open on my laptop right now). To say I was taken aback would be an understatement. Here was an official company representative telling a journalist they would need to hand over the final edit to the corporation overseen and co-founded by the interviewee. Brazen doesn't begin to cover it. Scurrilous just about might.

Related:Update: Last Flag dev Night Street Games lays off 'about a dozen' staff

This should go without saying, but that is a wildly insulting request to make of any journalist. One that immediately calls into question their creditability and professionalism. Imagine if we had pressed ahead with the interview without ever disclosing what we sacrificed to secure access? Why would readers ever trust us—or indeed me—again? Absurd. Farcical. Deliriously barmpot. 

In response, I very politely explained that we would not permit Embracer to edit and approve coverage before publication, but stressed I was still keen on the interview. I never did hear back after that. I cannot imagine why—but I suppose Wingefors was a very busy man. 

He would, of course, be even busier around a year later. 

Following the collapse of a major investment into Embracer (reportedly tabled by Saudi-owned investment vehicle PIF), Wingefors kicked off what remains one of the most harrowing layoff initiatives in recent memory. The company commenced a seemingly never-ending round of sweeping job cuts, studio closures, project cancellations, and divestments that in the mind of any sane, rational person would represent a gargantuan leadership failure. 

To suggest Embracer's impact on the game industry over the past few years has been anything close to positive would be to betray the very idea of reality itself. It would be like describing the maiden voyage of the Titanic as a moderate success because, well, technically some people made it to New York. 

Related:Quantic Dream cutting jobs after scrapping Spellcasters Chronicles

Yet, that is precisely what Wingefors is attempting to do. After spending years feeding workers and beloved franchises into the meat grinder, Wingefors last year stepped back as CEO to become chair of the board at Embracer Group in its new guise of four separate publicly-traded companies in a trenchcoat. 

In an open letter published earlier this week, Wingefors walked investors through the upcoming spin off of Fellowship Entertainment, a new publicly-traded video game and entertainment segment that might eventually be trusted to make some layoffs of its own. He also took the opportunity to embark upon what feels like the opposite of an apology tour.

"Ever since the foundation of Embracer the question has been asked to me what the strategy of the group really is. The reality is that it has been difficult to easily communicate the Embracer story historically due to its mix of operational strategies and a combination of decentralization from HQ but part-centralization within operating groups," he wrote, perhaps overlooking the fact that it has also been difficult to communicate Embracer's strategy because it has largely consisted of stepping on rakes with a level of precision and finesse that would put a Cirque du Soleil performer to shame.

Related:Ubisoft's net bookings are down 54% year-on-year

Wingefors goes on to acknowledge that "for some, we have become closely associated with industry layoffs," which feels like a gross understatement when you consider this is one of the rare occasions in history when the name of a video game corporation and its co-founder has become synonymous with the kiss of death. Bobby Kotick perhaps led the way in that regard.

"The reality is that we have worked hard to retain as many people as possible through a very difficult period, while balancing the needs to drive a profitable business operation," added Wingefors. "That reflects our commitment to our people, our portfolio of beloved IPs, and the promises we have made to entrepreneurs. Looking back at the large adjustment we made in 2023, we decided not to do a hard 'US Corporate style' headcount reduction, but to give a number of studios and IPs the chance to prove themselves." 

Where do you even begin with this one? Using the phrase 'tone deaf' might actually be tone deaf. Correct me if I’m wrong, but it feels like Wingefors is suggesting that Embracer—a conglomerate that swallowed studios whole in pursuit of massive investment before torching the careers of thousands of workers—actually cares about its employees and even did them a massive solid by giving them a chance to "prove themselves" in what I can only imagine felt like a legally distinct version of the Hunger Games (I don't think Embracer managed to buy that IP). 

You wonder what the folks over at Crystal Dynamics, Eidos Montreal, Tripwire Interactive, Free Radical, Volition, 3D Realms, and the reams of other studios decimated by their benevolent parent company might say in response. 

Wingefors presumably felt they were on a roll at this point, because some of his next musings are legitimately bonkers. "Cost control does not get enough respect, but over time it makes a bigger difference than many people realize," he suggested in a remarkable moment of hubris. 

In this instance, I begrudgingly have to agree. Cost control does make a bigger difference than people realize. For example, it helped the company bid adieu to over 4,500 employees and cancel 80 projects during the fiscal year ended March, 2024. Truly, a staggering accomplishment we haven't celebrated enough. 

I haven't even gotten to the kicker yet. You might imagine that after proving wholeheartedly that unchecked M&A might not actually be the smartest play in the book, Wingefors is preparing to turn over a new leaf and atone for smashing through the game industry with a level of panache only matched by Miley Cyrus and her fabled Wrecking Ball. Think again. 

"I can once again also see the potential for value accretive, opportunistic, and synergistic M&A to strengthen these already successful niche businesses within the Embracer business segment," continued Wingefors. "To be clear on M&A, it could be all three—acquisitions, mergers with others and divestments of less strategic assets." 

There you have it. A bold commitment to repeating the transgressions of the past—or rather, present, given the Embracer layoffs are still ongoing—all while suggesting he's actually done a solid job at steering the company though a crisis of his own making. 

It's one thing to make a mistake. We are all fallible. But what's happening here is appalling revisionism. A refusal to accept even an ounce of responsibility for treating the livelihoods of industry workers like a corporate plaything. "Trust matters more than anything," stated Wingefors in his letter. Irony has lost all meaning.

About the Author

Senior Editor, News, GameDeveloper.com

Game Developer news editor Chris Kerr is an award-winning reporter with over a decade of experience in the game industry. His byline has appeared in notable print and digital publications including Edge, Stuff, Wireframe, International Business Times, and PocketGamer.biz. Throughout his career, Chris has covered major industry events including GDC, PAX Australia, Gamescom, Paris Games Week, and Develop Brighton. 



https://www.gamedeveloper.com/business/opinion-lars-wingefors-is-out-of-his-mind
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: LATAM indie devs lay out how to improve your text animation
item.content:encoded:
How do you get players to read your game's text?

Developers working on narrative and non-narrative games alike have struggled with this question over and over again in game development history. It's an agonizing dilemma. You put all that work into your game's story, your flavor text, and especially your tutorial sequences—only for their eyes to glaze over as they mash the "advance" button and barely read any of it.

This phenomenon was on display during our recent visit to Gamescom LATAM. Across the event, an astonishingly talented cohort of independent game developers showed off games with lush visuals and finely-tuned mechanics—but one major feature made some incredibly attractive, and others less engaging: text animation.

As we've belabored before, your game's text animation plays a major role in telling your story or even just getting players to internalize your tutorials. It's also an aspect that can make your game feel "cheap" even if other elements are polished to a fine sheen. 

Related:Donkey Kong Bananza has a big-brained approach to asset recycling

As the developers we spoke with discussed, once you internalize that notion, a whole playground opens up to make your text feel like a part of the game world, offer commentary on events, or elegantly guide the player's eye better than a ham-fisted tutorial can.

Climb out of Hell leaves a smoldering impression

Retro-inspired side-scrolling platformers and top-down adventure games were the most popular genres across the three booths showcasing work from LATAM developers. This wasn't necessarily surprising. Developers we spoke with largely said they were using Unreal Engine, Gamemaker, and Godot, and both Unity and Gamemaker were present at the show, pitching their tech to local devs. But these developers weren't just retreading old ground. Each had their own flavorful spin with gameplay never in the SNES or Sega Genesis or Game Boy Advance games that inspired them.

Solo developer Luca Signorini's Climb Out of Hell (being released under the banner of Uendy Studios) was one such example. Signorini explained to Game Developer that he wanted to take the core gameplay system of Angry Birds—launching the player character forward by dragging the mouse or finger—and use it to fuel a lost soul's treacherous climb out of Hell.

Text in Climb out of Hell doesn't just appear onscreen, it slowly smolders, like lava oozing down a mountain or fire working its way through a piece of paper. Signorini explained that he used Unity's basic UI toolkit to make this text effect. "I have an asset called 'gamefeel,'" he said, pointing the fiery effect onscreen. "I use 'gamefeel' to make the text appear onscreen in slow motion and fade in and fade out, with a bit of a delay so players have time to take in what the text shows."

Related:Tencent's genAI animation tools don't seem to make games more fun

He said he was inspired by the classic definition of "juicing" in game animation, just applying it to text instead of gunfire, action, or motion. Climb out of Hell mixes traditional iconography of the underworld with some other unique inspirations, but the pleasant sensation of watching the text scroll did the most work in encouraging me to try, fail, and try again to escape the halls of the damned.

Artius: Pure Imagination embraces text as art

PVic's Artius: Pure Imagination is a feast for the eyes and an experience that speaks to every artist threatened by tech bros' promise to replace human creativity with genAI art. Gabriel Casanova, one of the games' developers, told us the game's art is inspired by the art of Brazilian cartoonist Ziraldo. Ziraldo's comics often followed energetic kids, capturing their excitement with buoyant text along with bright colors and exaggerated shapes. 

Artius is made in Gamemaker, and Casanova said the team uses a specific text renderer called "Scribble." "It's an amazing library which lets you do all kinds of things with text. We made our own fonts for the game and used Scribble to create custom animations to manipulate the text. We can shrink it, change the color, and have certain individual animations for certain letters. 

Casanova also raised the topic of localization when quizzed about the letter-by-letter animation choice, saying it better accommodates languages like Chinese and Japanese because of their use of special individual characters."

It's probably no surprise that a game about celebrating the arts takes such care with its visuals, right down to the text, but Casanova's tip of the hat to localization shows how the translation process can be made easier when you consider it further ahead in development. But maybe they had reason to think of it sooner—PVic and most other developers at the show supported both Portuguese and English in their games, accommodating local players and us international travelers (some of whom were publishers looking for games to sign) alike.

Ghostless places words precisely on the screen

Posted up at the Abragames booth, Coffeenauts developer Josiel R.A Silva was closely watching attendees play Ghostless, a side-scrolling science fiction post-apocalyptic game that blends the metroidvania and colony sim genres. Of the developers we spoke with, he was the one paying the most attention to how players responded to text animation. That's because according to him, Coffenauts had come to Gamescom LATAM to explicitly test Ghostless' tutorial sequence.

(Coffeenauts hasn't posted any video of Ghostless online yet, so we can't show you any videos of it in action).

Because Ghostless blends two complex game systems—one that rewards quick action and exploration and another that emphasizes resource management—teaching players how to pick it up is difficult. In previous tests, players were missing large amounts of information. 

As we spoke, a player was watching text scroll across the screen one letter at a time in an unskippable text sequence, animated to resemble how text appears while typing in a retro computer terminal.

"We chose to display the text this way so the player can see the text but not immediately read all of it," he said, noting that they'd built a coroutine in Unity to mete out the text letter-by-letter in a designated timeframe. What Coffeenauts did discover, he said, was that where the text was placed onscreen mattered as much as how it was animated and the information it contained.

As we spoke, a player progressing through the tutorial interacted with a non-playable character whose dialogue was animated in the middle of the screen, floating above her head. But the tutorial information Silva was focusing on appeared in a text box on the bottom of the screen. This is because, according to Silva, how text appears relative to gameplay can change how players feel about the game.

"When you have a tutorial in the middle of the screen, players feel you're blocking their gameplay," he said. "Text on the bottom of the screen doesn't block the player's control."

By contrast, text in the middle of the screen—be it dialogue, journal entries, or other elements that emerge from gameplay—is text they were "waiting" to see. Or put in other words, it's text that appears as an outcome of the player's behavior, and therefore more easily fits in the flow of gameplay instead of interrupting it.

[AFANTASIA] makes meaning with fonts and position

Finally, we have Afantasia, the game that probably made the most complex use of text animation across all of Gamescom LATAM. This surreal psychological adventure comes from solo developer Leon Stevens, operating under the studio name "noidnoidnoid." 

The game follows a young boy who "bargained his memories for the ability to imagine," and now wanders through his hometown, rendered in a variety of pixelated visuals. He weaves through the town, re-living the memories of the people (and sometimes animals) who lived there. Each sequence makes use of a different style of gameplay and art direction, and in the regular gameplay sequences, the player character has interior conversations with his own psyche, guided by Jungian-inspired choices in a dialogue tree. 

Most importantly for our purposes—its text animation is constantly changing throughout each of those sequences.

For instance, when the player character is speaking with his inner self in the general 2D adventure sequences, text appears above the character's head and towards the upper portion of the screen, each side of the conversation shown in different colors. But in one memory sequence where the player is embodying a dog's memory in a first-person view, text appears both in the environment itself and in a black letterbox bar on the side of the screen.

Text appears in different positions on the screen, uses different fonts, colors, and position to sell [AFANTASIA]'s surreal story. "Each [text] character is a component in the user interface," Stevens explained. The text is produced through Motion Design in Unreal Engine.

"My system is based on data tables. Each letter spawns a component that has independent animations and they start to offset randomly. Each letter is animated by a curve that translates the position at a random time in that curve. I instantiate each character as a component, and each component is animated."

The biggest challenge to working this way, he said, is to make sure players know when text has ended and they can progress forward. That meant making sentence-ending punctuation like ellipses, exclamation points, and question marks as visible as possible. 

Stevens' work on [AFANTASIA] captured the power of what we saw across these and a number of other games: text isn't just an obligation, it's a dynamic system that can be as interactive or as inert as you want it, and it's a vast open playground for developers to explore artistic intent or A/B test features that drive player retention.

Onscreen, his protagonist was waxing poetic about the nature of existence and emotions. But his explanation for making these different choices boiled down to one sentence: "I really, really like to play with different UI positions."

Game Developer was invited to Gamescom Latam by event partner Abragames, which covered flights and accommodation.

About the Author

Senior Editor, GameDeveloper.com

Bryant Francis is a writer, journalist, and narrative designer based in Boston, MA. He currently writes for Game Developer, a leading B2B publication for the video game industry. His credits include Proxy Studios' 4X strategy game Zephon, Iron Anchor Studios' Down With The Ship, and Amplitude Studio's 2017 game Endless Space 2.



https://www.gamedeveloper.com/art/latam-indie-devs-lay-out-how-to-improve-your-text-animation
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Quantic Dream cutting jobs after scrapping Spellcasters Chronicles
item.content:encoded:
Chris Kerr,

Senior Editor, News,GameDeveloper.com

May 21, 2026

2 Min Read

Quantic Dream has discontinued development on Spellcasters Chronicles and is now preparing to make layoffs. The French publisher launched the free-to-play strategy title into early access in February, but said the multiplayer experience failed to find an audience. 

"With this project, our teams set out to explore new creative territories and create a bold, original multiplayer experience. Spellcasters Chronicles allowed us to experiment with new concepts and bring to life an ambitious game shaped by the talent, creativity, and dedication of everyone involved," reads a statement shared on LinkedIn.  

"However, in today's particularly challenging market environment, the game has not reached the audience needed to ensure its long-term sustainability. We have therefore made the difficult decision to refocus our efforts on our other projects."

Quantic Dream has initiated an "internal reorganization" as a result of the cancellation that will result in some job cuts. "We are fully committed to handling this transition with fairness, care, and respect, and will prioritize internal reassignments wherever possible to support our other productions," added the company.

Related:Update: Last Flag dev Night Street Games lays off 'about a dozen' staff

The studio said development of upcoming action-adventure title Star Wars Eclipse has not been impacted by the decision and is continuing as planned.

In response to the news, French labor union Le Syndicat des Travailleureuses du Jeu Vidéo (STJV) described the development of Spellcasters Chronicles as a "colossal risk" that could now cost almost 100 workers their jobs. 

The union said 95 roles could be impacted by layoffs related to the cancellation, and claimed studio leaders have failed to take accountability for mismanaging the project.

"Over all these years, nobody questioned the business model or how the game was to become profitable. Catastrophic project management resulted in iteration after iteration, exhausting the team and leading production straight to disaster," reads a statement issued by STJV. 

"Quantic Dream leadership points to external factors; we blame their decisions, be they financial, creative or organizational. The resulting project was ungodly expensive and aimed at a high-risk market, without matching current player demands."

STJV said nobody within Quantic Dream is surprised by the cancellation, despite being told that success was a foregone conclusion for Spellcasters Chronicles by project leaders. 

"Worker representatives have sounded the alarm about the project's colossal risk level numerous times. In front of employees, management arrogantly explained that success was a given for Spellcasters Chronicles, thanks to '30 years of experience' of the decision-makers," the union added. "In front of the workers’ council, management refused to consider other scenarios and to plan accordingly. Failure was never an option, never thought about, never planned for: incompetence led us here today. Workers pay for management’s misguided ways."

Related:Opinion: Lars Wingefors is out of his mind

STJV is demanding that Quantic Dream and parent company NetEase cancel the planned terminations and reassign all members of the Spellcasters team to Star Wars Eclipse. The union has also called on all management and creative directors "responsible for this failure" to resign immediately.

Game Developer has reached out to Quantic Dream for comment. 

About the Author

Senior Editor, News, GameDeveloper.com

Game Developer news editor Chris Kerr is an award-winning reporter with over a decade of experience in the game industry. His byline has appeared in notable print and digital publications including Edge, Stuff, Wireframe, International Business Times, and PocketGamer.biz. Throughout his career, Chris has covered major industry events including GDC, PAX Australia, Gamescom, Paris Games Week, and Develop Brighton. 



https://www.gamedeveloper.com/business/quantic-dream-cutting-jobs-after-scrapping-spellcasters-chronicles
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
